name: balado bakso
curator_note: A meeting point between Minangkabau chilli heat and Hokkien meatball tradition. The Hokkien bakso (bak = meat, so = ball) travelled with Chinese migrants and became something entirely Indonesian. What we call bak chor mee in Singapore has a cousin here that went a different direction.
word_origin: Hokkien: bak (肉, meat) + so (鬆, loose/minced)

name: nian gao
curator_note: The translation 'glutinous rice cake' is technically accurate and completely misses the point. Nian gao (年糕) carries the homophone nian (年, year) and gao (高, tall/high) — the name IS the meaning. To translate it is to lose the entire reason people eat it at New Year. Some names should travel as themselves.
word_origin: Mandarin: 年 (nián, year) + 糕 (gāo, cake) — homophone with 年高, year-high, a prosperity wish

name: chee cheong fun
curator_note: Cheung fun (腸粉) in Cantonese literally means intestine noodle — named for the shape of the rolled rice sheet, not the contents. Singaporean Cantonese communities shortened and softened it into chee cheong fun. The Singaporean version diverged significantly — sweet sauce, prawn paste, hae bee hiam — while Hong Kong kept it minimal and steamed. Same name, different dish. That divergence is the story.
word_origin: Cantonese: 猪肠粉 (zhū cháng fěn) — pig intestine flour/noodle, named for shape
